Elements: be, have, do, can, could, may, might, must, shall, should, will, would, ought
Notes: We have to investigate “ought to” constructions, because they can do inversion but
I’d actually be tempted to make inflection on auxiliaries a biuniqueness issue.

Elements: can, could, may, might, must, shall, should, will, would

Example: They would [probably] intentionally have done it.
They would probably [intentionally] have done it.
He has probably not [intentionally] done it.
He has probably not intentionally [completely] destroyed the city.

Notes: Are the forms of “be” biuniqueness or constituency?

Example: The city was [probably] destroyed by him.
The city was probably not [intentionally] destroyed by him.

Elements: baby, house, pet, dog, child, nanny, grounds, care, hand, breast, lip, eye, name, sound, fact, spell, cross, strong, rough, stone, air, spin, pressure
dis-, mis-, up-, down-, fore-, co-, en-, ex-, sub-, pro-, per-, sur-, contra-, ab-, ad-, ob-, sup-, cata-, dia-, epi-, para-, se-, tele-, ante-, circum-, eu-, inter-, intra-, syn-, be-,

Notes: Adam has three slots using the Item-Arrangement model, I’d go with deviations from biuniqueness

Example: It is tough for them to pick [the ball up].
It is tough for them to pick up the ball [intentionally].
It is tough for them to pick up the ball [inside in the room.]
It is tough for them to pick up the ball in the room [tomorrow probably when they don’t even know what they are doing].
